20.12: Amasa was lying, wallowing in his blood, in the middle of the road. The man saw that all the troops were stopping there, so he dragged Amasa off the road into the field and threw a cloth over him, since everyone who came near him stopped.
20.13: Once he was off the road, every man went on after Joab to pursue Sheba son of Bichri.
20.14: Sheba passed through all the tribes of Israel to Abel Beth-maacah, and all the Bichrites assembled and followed him there.
20.15: Joab's forces came and laid siege to him at Abel Beth-maacah. They built a siege ramp against the city, and it stood against the outer wall. They were battering the wall to bring it down.
20.16: Then a wise woman in the city called out, 'Listen! Listen! Tell Joab to come near so I can speak to him.'
20.17: He came near her. The woman asked, 'Are you Joab?' He answered, 'I am.' She said, 'Hear the words of your servant.' He replied, 'I am listening.'
20.18: She said, 'In the old days people used to say, "Get your answer at Abel," and that is how they settled things.'
20.19: 'I am one of the peaceable and faithful in Israel. You are trying to destroy a city that is like a mother in Israel. Why do you want to swallow up the LORD's inheritance?'
20.20: Joab replied, 'Far be it from me, far be it indeed, that I should swallow up or destroy!'
20.21: 'That is not the case at all. But a man from the hill country of Ephraim, named Sheba son of Bichri, has raised his hand against King David. Hand him over—just him—and I will withdraw from the city.' The woman said to Joab, 'His head will be thrown to you over the wall.'
20.22: Then the woman in her wisdom went to all the people. They cut off the head of Sheba son of Bichri and threw it out to Joab. He blew the trumpet, and they dispersed from the city, each man going home. Joab returned to the king in Jerusalem.
20.23: Joab was over all the army of Israel; Benaiah son of Jehoiada was over the Cherethites and the Pelethites;
20.24: Adoram was in charge of forced labor; Jehoshaphat son of Ahilud was the royal recorder;
20.25: Sheva was the secretary; Zadok and Abiathar were the priests;
20.26: and Ira the Jairite was David's personal priest.

21.1: During David's reign there was a famine for three years in succession. David sought the LORD's face, and the LORD said, 'There is bloodguilt on Saul and his house because he put the Gibeonites to death.'
21.2: The king summoned the Gibeonites and spoke to them. The Gibeonites were not Israelites but a remnant of the Amorites; the Israelites had sworn to spare them, yet Saul in his zeal for Israel and Judah had tried to wipe them out.
21.3: David said to the Gibeonites, 'What should I do for you? How shall I make atonement, so that you will bless the LORD's inheritance?'
21.4: The Gibeonites answered him, 'It is not a question of silver or gold between us and Saul or his house, and it is not ours to put any man to death in Israel.' He said, 'Whatever you say, I will do for you.'
21.5: They said to the king, 'The man who destroyed us and planned to annihilate us so that we would have no place in all Israel's territory—
21.6: 'let seven of his male descendants be handed over to us, so we may hang them before the LORD at Gibeah of Saul, the LORD's chosen.' The king said, 'I will hand them over.'
21.7: But the king spared Mephibosheth son of Jonathan son of Saul, because of the oath sworn before the LORD between David and Jonathan son of Saul.
21.8: The king took Armoni and Mephibosheth, the two sons of Rizpah daughter of Aiah, whom she had borne to Saul, and the five sons of Merab daughter of Saul, whom she had borne to Adriel son of Barzillai the Meholathite.
21.9: He handed them over to the Gibeonites, who hanged them on the mountain before the LORD. All seven fell together. They were put to death in the first days of harvest, at the beginning of the barley harvest.
21.10: Then Rizpah daughter of Aiah took sackcloth and spread it on a rock for herself. She kept watch from the beginning of harvest until rain fell on them from the sky, keeping the birds away by day and the wild animals away by night.
21.11: David was told what Rizpah daughter of Aiah, Saul's concubine, had done.
21.12: David went and retrieved the bones of Saul and his son Jonathan from the men of Jabesh-gilead, who had secretly taken them from the public square of Beth-shan, where the Philistines had hung them on the day they killed Saul at Gilboa.
21.13: He brought up from there the bones of Saul and Jonathan, and they also gathered the bones of those who had been hanged.
21.14: They buried the bones of Saul and his son Jonathan in Zela of Benjamin, in the tomb of his father Kish. They carried out everything the king commanded. After that, God answered prayer on behalf of the land.
21.15: There was war again between the Philistines and Israel. David and his men went down and fought the Philistines. David became exhausted.
21.16: Ishbi-benob, one of the descendants of the giants, whose bronze spearhead weighed three hundred shekels and who carried a new sword, intended to kill David.
21.17: But Abishai son of Zeruiah came to his aid, struck the Philistine, and killed him. Then David's men swore to him, 'You must not go out with us to battle again, or you will put out the lamp of Israel.'
21.18: After this there was another battle with the Philistines at Gob. At that time Sibbecai the Hushathite struck down Saph, who was one of the descendants of the giants.
21.19: There was another battle with the Philistines at Gob, and Elhanan son of Jaare-oregim the Bethlehemite struck down Goliath the Gittite, whose spear shaft was like a weaver's beam.
21.20: There was another battle at Gath, where there was a man of great size who had six fingers on each hand and six toes on each foot—twenty-four in all. He too was descended from the giants.
21.21: When he taunted Israel, Jonathan son of Shimei, David's brother, struck him down.
21.22: These four were descendants of the giants in Gath, and they fell by the hand of David and his servants.

22.1: David spoke to the LORD the words of this song on the day the LORD delivered him from the hand of all his enemies and from the hand of Saul.
22.2: He said: 'The LORD is my rock, my fortress, and my deliverer;
22.3: 'The God of my rock—in him I take refuge. He is my shield, the horn of my salvation, my stronghold, my refuge, my savior who saves me from violence.
22.4: 'I call on the LORD, who is worthy of praise, and I am saved from my enemies.
22.5: 'The waves of death engulfed me; the torrents of destruction terrified me;
22.6: 'the cords of Sheol wrapped around me; the snares of death set themselves before me.
22.7: 'In my distress I called to the LORD; I called to my God. From his temple he heard my voice, and my cry reached his ears.
22.8: 'Then the earth reeled and shook; the foundations of the heavens trembled and quaked, because he was angry.
22.9: 'Smoke went up from his nostrils, devouring fire from his mouth; blazing coals were kindled by it.
22.10: 'He parted the heavens and came down; dark storm clouds were under his feet.
22.11: 'He rode on a cherub and flew; he appeared on the wings of the wind.
22.12: 'He made darkness his canopy around him—a gathering of waters, thick storm clouds.
22.13: 'Out of the brightness before him, coals of fire blazed forth.
22.14: 'The LORD thundered from heaven; the Most High raised his voice.
22.15: 'He shot his arrows and scattered them; lightning bolts and threw them into disorder.
22.16: 'Then the channels of the sea were exposed and the foundations of the world laid bare, at the LORD's rebuke, at the blast of the breath of his nostrils.
22.17: 'He reached down from on high and took me; he drew me out of many waters.
22.18: 'He rescued me from my powerful enemy, from those who hated me—for they were stronger than I.
22.19: 'They confronted me in the day of my disaster, but the LORD was my support.
22.20: 'He brought me out into a spacious place; he rescued me, because he delighted in me.
22.21: 'The LORD rewarded me according to my righteousness; according to the cleanness of my hands he repaid me.
22.22: 'For I have kept the ways of the LORD and have not turned wickedly from my God.
22.23: 'For all his ordinances were before me, and I did not turn away from his statutes.
22.24: 'I was blameless before him and kept myself from wrongdoing.
22.25: 'So the LORD has repaid me according to my righteousness, according to my cleanness in his sight.
22.26: 'To the faithful you show yourself faithful; to the blameless you show yourself blameless;
22.27: 'to the pure you show yourself pure, but with the devious you show yourself shrewd.
22.28: 'You deliver a humble people, but your eyes are on the proud to bring them low.
22.29: 'For you are my lamp, O LORD, and the LORD lights up my darkness.
22.30: 'By your help I can charge a troop; by my God I can leap over a wall.
22.31: 'As for God, his way is perfect; the word of the LORD is flawless. He is a shield for all who take refuge in him.
22.32: 'For who is God except the LORD? And who is a rock besides our God?
22.33: 'God is my strong refuge and has made my way blameless.
22.34: 'He made my feet like the feet of a deer and set me secure on the heights.
22.35: 'He trains my hands for battle so that my arms can draw a bow of bronze.
22.36: 'You have given me the shield of your salvation, and your condescension has made me great.
22.37: 'You gave a wide path for my feet so that my ankles did not give way.
22.38: 'I pursued my enemies and destroyed them; I did not turn back until I had consumed them.
22.39: 'I consumed them; I struck them through so that they could not rise; they fell beneath my feet.
22.40: 'For you armed me with strength for battle; you made those who rose against me bow down under me.
22.41: 'You made my enemies turn and flee; I destroyed those who hated me.
22.42: 'They looked for help, but there was no one to save; they cried to the LORD, but he did not answer them.
22.43: 'I ground them fine as dust before the wind; I crushed and stamped them down like the mire in the streets.
22.44: 'You delivered me from the quarrels of my people; you kept me as the head of nations; a people I had not known served me.
22.45: 'Foreigners came cringing to me; as soon as they heard of me they obeyed me.
22.46: 'Foreigners lost heart and came trembling from their strongholds.
22.47: 'The LORD lives! Blessed be my Rock! Exalted be my God, the Rock of my salvation,
22.48: 'the God who gives me vengeance and brings down peoples under me,
22.49: 'who brought me out from my enemies. You exalted me above those who rose against me; you rescued me from the violent man.
22.50: 'Therefore I will praise you, O LORD, among the nations, and sing praises to your name.
22.51: 'He gives great victories to his king and shows steadfast love to his anointed—to David and his descendants forever.'

23.1: These are the last words of David: The declaration of David son of Jesse, the declaration of the man God raised to prominence, the anointed of the God of Jacob, the beloved singer of Israel's psalms:
23.2: 'The Spirit of the LORD speaks through me; his word is on my tongue.
23.3: 'The God of Israel has spoken; the Rock of Israel has said to me: Whoever rules over men with justice, who rules in the fear of God,
23.4: 'he is like the light of morning, like sunrise on a cloudless day, like the gleam of rain on green grass after showers.
23.5: 'Is it not true that my house stands so before God? For he has made with me an everlasting covenant, ordered and secured in every way. Will he not indeed bring about all my salvation and all my desire?
23.6: 'But worthless men are like thorns that are cast away, for they cannot be taken up with the hand;
23.7: 'whoever touches them must be armed with iron and the shaft of a spear, and they are utterly burned up where they stand.'
23.8: These are the names of David's mighty men: Josheb-basshebeth the Tahchemonite, chief of the Three. He raised his spear against eight hundred men and killed them in a single battle.
23.9: Next to him among the three warriors was Eleazar son of Dodo son of Ahohi. He was with David when they taunted the Philistines gathered for battle at Pas-dammim. When the men of Israel retreated,
23.10: he held his ground and struck down Philistines until his hand grew tired and his hand froze to the sword. The LORD brought about a great victory that day. The men returned after him only to strip the slain.
23.11: Next was Shammah son of Agee the Hararite. The Philistines had gathered at Lehi, where there was a field full of lentils. The men of Israel fled from the Philistines,
23.12: but he planted himself in the middle of the field, defended it, and struck down the Philistines. The LORD brought about a great victory.
23.13: Three of the thirty chief warriors went down at harvest time and came to David at the cave of Adullam, while a Philistine raiding band was encamped in the Valley of Rephaim.
23.14: David was in the stronghold at the time, and the Philistine garrison was at Bethlehem.
23.15: David was overcome with longing and said, 'O that someone would bring me water to drink from the well at Bethlehem's gate!'
23.16: Then the three warriors broke through the Philistine lines, drew water from the well at Bethlehem's gate, and carried it back to David. But he refused to drink it. He poured it out to the LORD
23.17: and said, 'Far be it from me, O LORD, to do this! Is this not the blood of the men who went at the risk of their own lives?' So he would not drink it. These were the things the three warriors did.
23.18: Abishai, the brother of Joab son of Zeruiah, was head of the Thirty. He raised his spear against three hundred men, killed them, and earned a name alongside the Three.
23.19: He was the most honored of the Thirty and became their commander, but he did not attain to the Three.
23.20: Benaiah son of Jehoiada was a valiant warrior from Kabzeel, a man of great deeds. He struck down two warriors of Moab. He also went down and killed a lion in a pit on a day when snow had fallen.
23.21: He also struck down a formidable Egyptian. The Egyptian had a spear in his hand, but Benaiah went against him with a club, snatched the spear from his hand, and killed him with his own spear.
23.22: These were the things Benaiah son of Jehoiada did, and he won a name alongside the three warriors.
23.23: He was more honored than the Thirty, but he did not attain to the Three. David placed him in charge of his bodyguard.
23.24: Asahel the brother of Joab was one of the Thirty; Elhanan son of Dodo of Bethlehem,
23.25: Shammah of Harod, Elika of Harod,
23.26: Helez the Paltite, Ira son of Ikkesh of Tekoa,
23.27: Abiezer of Anathoth, Mebunnai the Hushathite,
23.28: Zalmon the Ahohite, Maharai of Netophah,
23.29: Heleb son of Baanah of Netophah, Ittai son of Ribai of Gibeah of Benjamin,
23.30: Benaiah of Pirathon, Hiddai of the wadis of Gaash,
23.31: Abi-albon the Arbathite, Azmaveth of Bahurim,
23.32: Eliahba the Shaalbonite, the sons of Jashen, Jonathan,
23.33: Shammah the Hararite, Ahiam son of Sharar the Hararite,
23.34: Eliphelet son of Ahasbai of Maacah, Eliam son of Ahithophel the Gilonite,
23.35: Hezro of Carmel, Paarai the Arbite,
23.36: Igal son of Nathan of Zobah, Bani the Gadite,
23.37: Zelek the Ammonite, Naharai of Beeroth—the armor-bearer of Joab son of Zeruiah—
23.38: Ira the Ithrite, Gareb the Ithrite,

24.1: Again the LORD's anger was kindled against Israel, and he incited David against them, saying, 'Go, take a census of Israel and Judah.'
24.2: The king said to Joab, the army commander who was with him, 'Go through all the tribes of Israel from Dan to Beersheba and take a census of the people, so I may know how many there are.'
24.3: But Joab said to the king, 'May the LORD your God add to the people a hundredfold—enough for my lord the king to see with his own eyes. But why does my lord the king want to do this?'
24.4: But the king's command overruled Joab and the army commanders. Joab and the army commanders went out from the king to count the people of Israel.
24.5: They crossed the Jordan and camped at Aroer, south of the city in the middle of the Gadite valley, and toward Jazer.
24.6: They went to Gilead and to Kadesh in the land of the Hittites; then they came to Dan, and from Dan they made their way around to Sidon,
24.7: and came to the fortified city of Tyre and all the cities of the Hivites and Canaanites. Then they went out into the Negeb of Judah at Beersheba.
24.8: When they had gone through the whole land, they came back to Jerusalem after nine months and twenty days.
24.9: Joab reported the census totals to the king: Israel had eight hundred thousand fighting men capable of bearing arms, and Judah had five hundred thousand.
24.10: After David had counted the people, his conscience struck him. David said to the LORD, 'I have sinned greatly in what I have done. Now, LORD, please take away the guilt of your servant, for I have acted very foolishly.'
24.11: When David got up in the morning, the word of the LORD had come to the prophet Gad, David's seer:
24.12: 'Go and tell David, "This is what the LORD says: I am offering you three options. Choose one of them and I will carry it out."'
24.13: Gad came to David and told him, saying, 'Shall three years of famine come on your land? Or will you flee three months before your enemies while they pursue you? Or shall there be three days of plague in your land? Now think it over and decide what answer I should give to the one who sent me.'
24.14: David said to Gad, 'I am in terrible distress. Let us fall into the hand of the LORD, for his mercy is great; but do not let me fall into the hand of man.'
24.15: So the LORD sent a plague on Israel from that morning until the end of the appointed time. From Dan to Beersheba, seventy thousand men died.
24.16: When the angel stretched out his hand toward Jerusalem to destroy it, the LORD relented from the disaster and said to the angel who was destroying the people, 'Enough! Stay your hand.' The angel of the LORD was at the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite.
24.17: When David saw the angel who was striking the people, he said to the LORD, 'I am the one who sinned, I the one who did wrong. These are but sheep—what have they done? Let your hand fall on me and my father's house.'
24.18: That day Gad came to David and said to him, 'Go up and build an altar to the LORD on the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite.'
24.19: David went up at Gad's word, just as the LORD had commanded.
24.20: Araunah looked up and saw the king and his servants approaching. He went out and bowed before the king with his face to the ground.
24.21: Araunah said, 'Why has my lord the king come to his servant?' David answered, 'To buy the threshing floor from you, to build an altar to the LORD, so that the plague may be lifted from the people.'
24.22: Araunah said to David, 'Let my lord the king take and offer whatever seems good to him. Here are the oxen for the burnt offering, and the threshing sledges and the ox yokes for wood.'
24.23: All this, O king, Araunah gives to the king. Araunah also said to the king, 'May the LORD your God be favorable to you.'
24.24: But the king replied to Araunah, 'No, I insist on buying it from you at full price. I will not offer to the LORD my God burnt offerings that cost me nothing.' David bought the threshing floor and the oxen for fifty shekels of silver.
24.25: David built an altar to the LORD there and offered burnt offerings and fellowship offerings. The LORD responded to the plea for the land, and the plague was lifted from Israel.
